FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study; N=9,066 children primarily aged 9-10 at baseline and 1-year follow-up

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis; models assessed psychiatric symptoms as predictors of screen time and screen time as predictors of psychiatric symptoms

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Social Psychiatry and Psychiatric Epidemiology (2024)

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Social screen time and nonsocial screen time are phone/SM-specific composites (measured as 'ordinally defined weekday and weekend time on social and nonsocial [e.g., YouTube] digital media'). Paired with individual-trait-level psychiatric symptoms (internalizing, externalizing, attention). Bidirectional results: psychiatric symptoms predict screen time (e.g., OR=1.22, 95% CI 1.22–1.23 for clinical problems predicting weekday nonsocial screen time) and screen time predicts psychiatric symptoms (e.g., higher social screen time associated with clinical externalizing, OR=1.14, 95% CI 1.13–1.15)

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported: OR estimates with 95% CIs (e.g., OR=1.22, OR=1.10, OR=1.65) for associations between psychiatric symptoms and screen time categories across weekday and weekend social and nonsocial use

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential longitudinal analysis with numeric results across multiple outcomes; controlled for baseline measures, demographic covariates (sex, age, race/ethnicity, income)

FT_EC7_reason: Design is longitudinal; however, SM/screen time is analyzed as both IV and DV in bidirectional models. Paper explicitly states bidirectionality: 'Comparable magnitude associations were observed for social and nonsocial media use predicting future psychiatric symptoms, suggesting bidirectionality.' When SM appears as IV in any analysis, FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: Paper reports bidirectional associations; psychiatric symptoms predict subsequent screen time patterns, and baseline screen time predicts future psychiatric symptoms. Both directions are supported by quantitative results.

FT_IC1.evidence: Utilizes the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) dataset with approximately 12,000 participants aged between 9 and 13 years old at baseline and 2-year follow-up

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using causal inference methods (Mendelian randomization, LME models, forward and reverse MR analysis) with both cross-sectional and longitudinal designs

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Advanced Science (2024)

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Screen use is measured with phone/SM-specific breakouts by modality: TVM (watching television shows/movies), Video (watching videos), Game (playing video games), Text (texting on cell phone), SNS (visiting social networking sites), VC (video chatting). Paired with individual-trait-level variables including cognition (picture vocabulary, flanker, pattern comparison, picture sequence memory, oral reading recognition), behavior (anxiety/depressed, withdrawn/depressed, somatic complaints, social problems, thought problems, attention problems, rule-breaking behavior, aggressive behavior), and brain volume in multiple regions. Results show direct and indirect causal associations (e.g., 'adverse causal effects of screen use on language ability and specific behaviors')

FT_IC3.evidence: Quantitative results from LME and MR analyses with β coefficients, p-values, and effect sizes reported. Table 1 shows associations between screen use types and cognitive/behavioral outcomes with correlations and p-values

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential causal analysis using Mendelian randomization and linear mixed effects models; reports numeric causal estimates with confidence intervals for direct and indirect effects of screen use on cognition, behavior, and brain volume

FT_EC7_reason: Design includes both cross-sectional analysis at baseline and longitudinal analysis at 2-year follow-up. Additionally, screen use is analyzed as IV in causal pathways (screen use → outcomes) as well as explored for reverse causation (outcomes → screen use). FT-EC7 does not apply when SM is IV in any analysis or when cross-sectional SM-as-DV analysis is present.
notes: Paper employs bidirectional causal inference; reports screen use causally influences brain development and behavioral outcomes, while also identifying reverse causation for rule-breaking and aggressive behaviors

FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study; n=6,143 adolescents (mean age 12 years at year 2), data from years 2, 3, and 4

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis using Bayesian hierarchical linear models to examine longitudinal associations between reward processing (year 2) and VGAQ scores over time

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Behavioral Addictions (2024)

FT_IC2.evidence: The paper measures Video Game Addiction Questionnaire (VGAQ) as the DV. Per v7 binding clarification: 'Problematic-use scales are SM/phone variables, not individual-trait outcomes.' VGAQ is a gaming addiction scale, not a smartphone or social media use variable. The paper does not isolate mobile/phone-based gaming, nor does it separately measure a qualifying phone/SM variable with its own coefficient. Binding rule states: 'Gaming alone does not qualify unless it is explicitly mobile/phone-based, OR the paper also separately measures a qualifying phone/SM variable with its own coefficient.' This paper measures gaming addiction scale only, paired with reward processing (neural activation in caudate nucleus). While reward processing is individual-trait-level, the exposure variable (VGAQ addiction scale) does not meet the FT-IC2 phone/SM requirement.

notes: Paper examines reward processing (fMRI activation in caudate during MID task) as predictor of gaming addiction symptoms (VGAQ). While the neuroimaging predictor is individual-trait-level, the absence of a qualifying phone/SM variable as the primary exposure disqualifies the paper at FT-IC2.
